seoTitle: How to Create and Work on Jira Tickets
metaDescription: Learn how to create a Jira ticket, fill in the key fields, and move it through your workflow from open to done in this quick walkthrough.
intro: Jira tickets are how teams track bugs, tasks, and stories from idea to completion. This guide walks through creating a new ticket and then working it through your project's workflow so nothing falls through the cracks.

steps.heading: Open your project and start a new ticket
steps.body: Navigate to your Jira project and use the create button to start a new issue. Choose the right issue type, such as task, bug, or story, before filling in details.

steps.heading: Fill in the ticket details
steps.body: Add a clear summary, a description of what needs to be done, and set the priority, assignee, and any labels or components that apply.

steps.heading: Add supporting information
steps.body: Attach files, link related issues, or add a due date so anyone who opens the ticket understands the context and urgency without asking around.

steps.heading: Move the ticket through your workflow
steps.body: Once created, update the status as work progresses, moving it from open to in progress and eventually to done, keeping your board an accurate reflection of reality.

steps.heading: Comment and collaborate
steps.body: Use comments to leave updates, ask questions, or tag teammates directly on the ticket so all discussion stays attached to the work itself.

faq.question: What is a Jira ticket?
faq.answer: A Jira ticket, or issue, is a single unit of work such as a bug, task, or user story that your team tracks from creation to completion.

faq.question: Can I edit a ticket after creating it?
faq.answer: Yes, you can edit the summary, description, assignee, and most other fields at any point in the ticket's lifecycle.

faq.question: How do I change who a ticket is assigned to?
faq.answer: Open the ticket and update the assignee field to reassign it to yourself or another team member.

faq.question: Can I link tickets together?
faq.answer: Yes, Jira lets you link related issues so dependencies and related work are easy to trace from any single ticket.
